nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs and lagoons across the Indo-West Pacific. Almost always found in association with large host sea anemones, most commonly carpet anemones (Stichodactyla haddoni, S. gigantea, S. mertensii) and bulb-tip/magnificent anemones (Entacmaea quadricolor, Heteractis magnifica, H. crispa), but also reported with Cryptodendrum adhaesivum, Macrodactyla doreensis, and other large anemone genera.

decorPreferences: a large host anemone (Stichodactyla spp. or Heteractis spp. preferred; Entacmaea quadricolor also accepted)
established live rock with crevices and shaded retreats for molting safety
moderate to strong flow — these are active filter feeders and benefit from particulates carried in the current
mature reef-quality water chemistry (the host anemone has stricter parameter needs than the crab itself)

diet.notes: An active filter feeder — uses fan-like setose maxillipeds to sweep planktonic particles from the water column. Also reported to scavenge mucus and small food scraps from its host anemone. In captivity the typical home reef is generally too clean and too gentle of flow to fully sustain it, so supplemental targeted feedings are commonly advised: small portions of frozen rotifers, baby brine shrimp, mysis, finely chopped marine flesh, marine snow, phytoplankton, or powdered/liquid coral foods squirted near the host anemone several times per week. Sources agree that under-feeding is the most common cause of slow decline in captivity.

goodWith: host anemones (Stichodactyla spp., Heteractis spp., Entacmaea quadricolor)
peaceful reef fish that do not harass invertebrates
cleaner shrimp (Lysmata spp.) and peppermint shrimp
snails and most reef-safe hermit crabs
avoidWith: other Neopetrolisthes individuals unless a confirmed mated pair — same-sex conspecifics have been reported to fight to the death over a shared anemone
clownfish sharing the same host anemone — competition for anemone real estate is common and clownfish typically dominate
predatory crabs (arrow crabs, large hermits, mithrax/emerald crabs in tight quarters)
shrimp-eating fish (large wrasses, hawkfish, lionfish, triggerfish, large groupers, puffers)
any copper-based medication in the tank's history

summary: A small, peaceful Indo-Pacific filter-feeding crab that lives commensally on large carpet or bulb-tip anemones. Generally considered reef-safe and hardy if a host anemone and supplemental feeding are provided.
careNotes: Neopetrolisthes maculatus is not a true crab but an anomuran in the family Porcellanidae, closely related to squat lobsters and hermit crabs. It is widely sold as both 'Neopetrolisthes maculatus' and 'Neopetrolisthes ohshimai' in the aquarium trade — Aqua Imports labels its stock N. ohshimai and LiveAquaria does the same. WoRMS and the modern taxonomic literature treat N. ohshimai (Miyake, 1937) as a junior subjective synonym of N. maculatus (H. Milne-Edwards, 1837); the spot-pattern variation that historically split them is no longer considered sufficient to separate species. Both attributions therefore refer to the same animal. Reported adult body length is around 2.5 cm carapace with a leg span up to ~5 cm. Water parameters cluster in the standard reef range: aquarium sources report temperature targets from roughly 22-27°C (averaged here to 23-27°C, with Aqua Imports' 23-26°C and LiveAquaria's 22-26°C agreeing closely with AquariumBreeder's and Reeflings' 24-27°C), pH 8.1-8.4, and salinity in the SG 1.023-1.026 band (Aqua Imports states 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026). Minimum tank-size guidance from retail sources is quite small (40-50 L), but because this species absolutely requires a healthy host anemone — and the anemone itself needs a mature, stable reef tank with strong lighting and flow — a 75 L minimum is used here as the more honest housing floor. The crab is a planktivorous filter feeder; home aquariums are commonly too clean and too gentle to sustain it from ambient particulates alone, so supplemental targeted feeding is generally advised several times per week. Temperament is peaceful toward almost everything except conspecifics: two N. maculatus of the same sex sharing one anemone have been reported to fight to the death, so a single specimen or a confirmed mated pair is the standard recommendation. Reef-safe with no documented coral or anemone damage — the relationship with the anemone is widely described as mutualistic (the crab cleans mucus and food scraps, the anemone provides predator protection via its stinging tentacles). Like all crustaceans, highly sensitive to copper-based medications. Magnesium, iodine, and trace-element dosing or routine reef-grade water changes are generally cited as supporting healthy molting. Escape risk is low — this species rarely leaves its host anemone.
breedingNotes: Has not been reliably bred in home aquariums. Females have been reported to carry around 600 reddish-brown eggs under the pleopods. Larval development is documented as two zoeal stages followed by one megalops stage; published rearing attempts have not gotten past the megalops transition, so successful captive larval rearing is essentially unreported. Sexing is possible via abdominal pleopod morphology (females broader) but generally difficult for non-specialists, so pairing is normally achieved by introducing two individuals and watching their interactions on the anemone — fighting indicates same-sex, while peaceful cohabitation suggests a male-female pair.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Primary per controller assignment. Lists the species as Neopetrolisthes ohshimai and gives temperature 74-78°F (≈23-26°C), pH 8.2-8.4, salinity 35 ppt (≈SG 1.026), origin Sri Lanka, reef-safe, peaceful with conspecific aggression, planktonic + anemone-mucus diet, and typical pair-with-host living. Silent on tank size, adult size, lifespan, and host-anemone specifics.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Porcelain Anemone Crab
additional.notes: Assigned secondary. Also labels the species N. ohshimai. Cross-checked: max size 3" (~7.6 cm leg span), temperature 72-78°F (22-26°C), pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, reef-safe with caution, easy care level, aggressive toward conspecifics, may compete with clownfish for the host anemone.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder — Porcelain Anemone Crab Detailed Guide
additional.notes: Used to fill fields the assigned sources omit: family Porcellanidae / order Decapoda (Anomura infraorder), adult size ~2.5 cm carapace / 6-8 cm leg span, lifespan 3-5 years, depth range 5-12 m, Indo-West Pacific country list, broader host-anemone list, ~600-egg clutch, zoeal/megalops larval staging, minimum tank size 40 L (treated as a low-end retail figure).

additional.name: Reeflings — Spotted Porcelain Crab Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: scientific name Neopetrolisthes maculatus with N. ohshimai, Petrolisthes ohshimai, Porcellana maculata as synonyms; adult size 5 cm; temperature 24-27°C; pH 8-8.5; SG 1.023-1.026; minimum tank 50 L; peaceful but territorial toward conspecifics; mutualistic with Entacmaea quadricolor and Heteractis magnifica; care level moderate-to-easy.

additional.name: WoRMS — Neopetrolisthes maculatus (H. Milne Edwards, 1837)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority used to confirm that Neopetrolisthes ohshimai Miyake, 1937 is a junior subjective synonym of Neopetrolisthes maculatus. Resolves the Aqua Imports / LiveAquaria N. ohshimai labelling vs. modern N. maculatus convention.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Sources do not give a typical molt interval for this species. Magnesium, iodine, and trace elements are commonly cited as supporting healthy molts in porcelain crabs; weekly trace-element dosing or routine reef-grade water changes generally cover this. The host anemone provides important post-molt cover during the soft-shell period; supplementary shaded crevices in live rock are generally advised. This species is also reported to use autotomy (voluntary leg shedding) as a defensive response, with lost limbs regrowing over successive molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females carry around 600 eggs; larval development includes two zoeal stages and a megalops stage. Published rearing attempts have not successfully reared larvae past the megalops transition, so home-aquarium breeding is essentially undocumented.
